title: Brain structural co-development is associated with internalizing symptoms two years later in the ABCD cohort.
abstract: BACKGROUND AND AIMS: About 1/3 of youth spend more than four hours/day engaged in screen media activity (SMA). This investigation utilized longitudinal brain imaging and mediation analyses to examine relationships among SMA, brain patterns, and internalizing problems. METHODS: Data from Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) participants with baseline and two-year follow-up structural imaging data that passed quality control (N = 5,166; 2,385 girls) were analyzed. Joint and Individual Variation Explained (JIVE) identified a brain co-development pattern among 221 brain features (i.e., differences in surface area, thickness, or cortical and subcortical gray-matter volume between baseline and two-year-follow-up data). Generalized linear mixed-effect models investigated associations between baseline SMA, structural co-development and internalizing and externalizing psychopathology at two-year follow-up. RESULTS: SMA at baseline was related to internalizing psychopathology at year 2 (Î²=0.020,SE=0.008,P=0.014) and a structural co-development pattern (Î²=0.015,SE=0.007,P=0.029), where the co-development pattern suggested that rates of change in gray-matter volumes of the brainstem, gray-matter volumes and/or cortical thickness measures of bilateral superior frontal, rostral middle frontal, inferior parietal, and inferior temporal regions were more similar than those in other regions. This component partially mediated the relationship between baseline SMA and future internalizing problems (indirect effect = 0.020, P-value = 0.043, proportion mediated: 2.24%). DISCUSSION AND CONCLUSIONS: Greater youth engagement in SMA at ages 9-10 years statistically predicted higher levels of internalizing two years later. This association was mediated by cortical-brainstem circuitry, albeit with relatively small effect sizes. The findings may help delineate processes contributing to internalizing behaviors and assist in identifying individuals at greater risk for such problems.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study (Release 3.0) baseline and 2-year follow-up data from 5,166 participants aged 9-10 years

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen media activity (SMA) measured at baseline via parent-report and child self-report questionnaires; specific types include TV, movies, online videos, video games, texting, social media

FT_IC3.evidence: Generalized linear mixed-effect models (GLMM) with mediation analyses; JIVE (Joint and Individual Variation Explained) identified brain co-development patterns; reports β coefficients, standard errors, and p-values

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric statistics reported: β=0.020 (SE=0.008, P=0.014) for SMA-internalizing association; β=0.015 (SE=0.007, P=0.029) for SMA-brain co-development; indirect effect=0.020, P=0.043

FT_IC5.evidence: Longitudinal empirical analysis with mediation modeling using baseline SMA and structural brain measures to predict 2-year follow-up internalizing problems

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in Journal of Behavioral Addictions, 2023

C1.FT_EC7_reason: Longitudinal design includes mediation/moderation (brain co-development pattern mediates SMA-internalizing link); also reports associations with psychological outcomes (internalizing/externalizing behaviors as dependent variables, not just SMA as DV)

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data: 'Data from Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) participants with baseline and two-year follow-up structural imaging data that passed quality control (N = 5,166)'

FT_IC2.evidence: Exposure is generic 'screen media activity (SMA)' described as 'About 1/3 of youth spend more than four hours/day engaged in screen media activity' with no breakdown into phone-specific or social-media-specific measures. No mention of smartphone, social media, texting, or other qualifying phone/SM variables.

FT_IC3.evidence: Does not meet FT_IC3 because FT_IC2 fails. SMA is not a qualifying phone/SM-specific exposure.

FT_IC4.evidence: Does not meet FT_IC4 because FT_IC2 fails. While numeric values are reported (β=0.020 for SMA→internalizing), the exposure variable is not qualifying.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: longitudinal cohort study with mediation analyses (GLMM) linking SMA at baseline to internalizing outcomes at 2-year follow-up with reported coefficients.

FT_IC6.evidence: Journal article published in Journal of Behavioral Addictions (2023); not a preprint, protocol, or duplicate.

C2.FT_EC7_reason: Does not apply because FT_IC2 fails (generic screen media activity, not phone/SM-specific). Therefore paper is excluded before FT-EC7 assessment.

title: The longitudinal association between reward processing and symptoms of video game addiction in the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study.
abstract: BACKGROUND AND AIMS: Video games are a common form of entertainment in adolescents, which may result in gaming habits characterized by impairment to reward-related decision-making. The aim of the current study was to investigate the relationship between reward processing and symptoms of gaming addiction in adolescents. METHODS: Data from three consecutive follow-up years (years 2, 3 and 4) of theÂ Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study were analyzed (n = 6,143, total observationsÂ = 12,745, mean age at year-2 = 12 years). Participants completed the Video Game Addiction Questionnaire (VGAQ) at each visit. Discrete stages of reward processing were measured at the year-2 visit using the Monetary Incentive Delay task while the participant completed a functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) scan. Bayesian hierarchical linear models were employed to examine the longitudinal association between reward processing in regions of interest at year-2 and VGAQ scores over time. RESULTS: Lower activation in the bilateral caudate during the anticipation of a large reward (Î² = -0.87, 95% CI: -1.68, -0.07) was associated with greater VGAQ scores over time. This implies that for each one-unit increase in brain activity in the caudate, there was an associated 0.87-point decrease in symptoms of gaming addiction as measured by the VGAQ. No association was found between reward feedback and VGAQ scores. DISCUSSION AND CONCLUSIONS: The findings suggest that abnormal reward processing in the caudate nucleus is associated with symptoms of gaming addiction in adolescents. These results provide a clearer understanding of the brain mechanisms involved in gaming addiction, which could inform future preventive and therapeutic strategies.

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, years 2-4 data, N=6,143 adolescents (mean age year-2=12 years), longitudinal follow-up design

FT_IC2.evidence: Paper measures video game addiction (VGAQ outcome) with reward processing (neural fMRI activation in caudate) as predictor. Video games are explicitly listed under NOT QUALIFYING exposures (E17: 'Console or PC video games' excluded). No smartphone or social media variable measured. Gaming addiction questionnaire is not a qualifying SM/phone exposure per criteria.

FT_IC3.evidence: Fails FT_IC2; exposure (reward processing neural activation) is neither smartphone nor social media

C1.FT_EC7_reason: Paper fails FT_IC2 (no smartphone/SM exposure); FT_EC7 inapplicable

FT_IC1.evidence: Abstract mentions ABCD Study but PDF not yet reviewed.

title: Passive Sensing of Preteens' Smartphone Use: An Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Cohort Substudy.
abstract: BACKGROUND: Concerns abound regarding childhood smartphone use, but studies to date have largely relied on self-reported screen use. Self-reporting of screen use is known to be misreported by pediatric samples and their parents, limiting the accurate determination of the impact of screen use on social, emotional, and cognitive development. Thus, a more passive, objective measurement of smartphone screen use among children is needed. OBJECTIVE: This study aims to passively sense smartphone screen use by time and types of apps used in a pilot sample of children and to assess the feasibility of passive sensing in a larger longitudinal sample. METHODS: The Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) study used passive, objective phone app methods for assessing smartphone screen use over 4 weeks in 2019-2020 in a subsample of 67 participants (aged 11-12 years; 31/67, 46% female; 23/67, 34% White). Children and their parents both reported average smartphone screen use before and after the study period, and they completed a questionnaire regarding the acceptability of the study protocol. Descriptive statistics for smartphone screen use, app use, and protocol feasibility and acceptability were reviewed. Analyses of variance were run to assess differences in categorical app use by demographics. Self-report and parent report were correlated with passive sensing data. RESULTS: Self-report of smartphone screen use was partly consistent with objective measurement (r=0.49), although objective data indicated that children used their phones more than they reported. Passive sensing revealed the most common types of apps used were for streaming (mean 1 hour 57 minutes per day, SD 1 hour 32 minutes), communication (mean 48 minutes per day, SD 1 hour 17 minutes), gaming (mean 41 minutes per day, SD 41 minutes), and social media (mean 36 minutes per day, SD 1 hour 7 minutes). Passive sensing of smartphone screen use was generally acceptable to children (43/62, 69%) and parents (53/62, 85%). CONCLUSIONS: The results of passive, objective sensing suggest that children use their phones more than they self-report. Therefore, use of more robust methods for objective data collection is necessary and feasible in pediatric samples. These data may then more accurately reflect the impact of smartphone screen use on behavioral and emotional functioning. Accordingly, the ABCD study is implementing a passive sensing protocol in the full ABCD cohort. Taken together, passive assessment with a phone app provided objective, low-burden, novel, informative data about preteen smartphone screen use.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study subsample. Methods: 'Four ABCD study sites that were roughly geographically dispersed... Four ABCD study sites participated in this pilot substudy project... 67 participants (aged 11-12 years)' from the larger ABCD cohort. Participants recruited as part of ABCD study 2-year follow-up.

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures specific smartphone use variables via passive sensing. Methods: 'passive, objective phone app methods for assessing smartphone screen use' including 'streaming (mean 1 hour 57 minutes per day... communication (mean 48 minutes per day... gaming (mean 41 minutes per day... and social media (mean 36 minutes per day).' Results: Table 1 lists streaming, YouTube, communication, gaming, social media, SMS, reading, music, and news as measured app categories.

FT_IC3.evidence: Reports correlation and inferential analyses. Results: 'Self-report of smartphone screen use was partly consistent with objective measurement (r=0.49)' with significance testing. Methods: 'Analyses of variance were run to assess differences in categorical app use by demographics.' Pearson correlations and paired sample t-tests reported.

FT_IC4.evidence: Reports correlation coefficients, means, standard deviations, and p-values. Results: 'r=0.49, P<.001' for self-report vs passive sensing correlation; Table 2 reports means (SD) for self-report and EARS app use; Figure 1 shows r=.49, P<.001 for parent-report correlation.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical measurement/validation study. Study design: feasibility and validation substudy within ABCD examining correspondence between passive sensing and self-report/parent-report of smartphone screen use, plus app use differences by demographics.

FT_IC6.evidence: Journal article published in JMIR Mental Health, 2021;8(10):e29426. Not a preprint, conference abstract, or duplicate.

C1.FT_EC7_reason: Cross-sectional measurement study comparing passive sensing to self-report and examining app use patterns by demographics. FT-EC7 requires longitudinal/prospective design with smartphone/SM as DV predicted by demographic-only variables. This paper is primarily a validation/feasibility study, not a longitudinal analysis of demographic predictors of screen time.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study, subsample of 67 participants (ages 11-12 years), pilot substudy described as part of ABCD implementation

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures passive sensing of smartphone screen use by app category (streaming, communication, gaming, social media). From abstract: 'passively sense smartphone screen use by time and types of apps used.' Table 1 breaks down app categories.

FT_IC3.evidence: Paper reports correlations between passive sensing data and self-report (r=0.49) and parent report (r=0.61), but these are measurement concordance tests, not associations between smartphone/SM and external non-SM outcomes. No inferential tests linking smartphone use to behavioral, mental health, or clinical outcomes reported. Per E5, measurement papers must also report phone/SM↔non-SM association; this paper only validates measurement agreement.

FT_IC4.evidence: Reports correlation coefficient r=0.49 (between passive sensing and self-report) and descriptive statistics (mean app use times in Table 1)

FT_IC5.evidence: This is a measurement/feasibility paper describing passive sensing protocol and validating EARS app concordance with self/parent report. No original analysis linking smartphone use to external outcomes. Per guideline: 'psychometric-only (factor structure / reliability without external outcome)' = NOT_MET

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article, JMIR Mental Health, 2021, DOI: 10.2196/29426

C2.FT_EC7_reason: Paper fails FT_IC5 (measurement-only without external outcome analysis); FT_EC7 directionality check is moot
